name: Anthropic ink-line plate
note: The Anthropic brand illustration look, as practiced on buildonanthropic.com and the campaign decks. Deliberately CHARACTERLESS: no storybook cast, because a plate is a diagrammatic gesture and not a scene from a story. Refs are the real published brand illustrations; the style IS the reference pack, so never generate from prose alone.

refMotifs.refs/hands-blocks.png: loopy hands arranging flat cream blocks
refMotifs.refs/hand-city-blocks.png: a hand over a skyline of cream blocks
refMotifs.refs/face-arcs-nodes.png: one-line profile face with radiating arcs and nodes
refMotifs.refs/hand-node-graph.png: a hand and a connected node graph
refMotifs.refs/hand-writing-paper.png: a hand working on a flat cream sheet
refMotifs.refs/hand-shapes-flow.png: a hand moving geometric shapes along arrows
refMotifs.refs/cursor-face-profile.png: a cursor, a hand, and a one-line profile face

styleLine: one continuous single-weight wobbly confident BLACK ink brush line, hand-drawn and imperfect like a marker doodle that never lifts; loopy long-fingered hands interacting with flat shapes; one-line abstract faces in profile, usually observing the work rather than doing it; flat solid ground with subtle paper grain; flat ivory-cream shapes as the only fills; generous negative space
rejectedPoles: visible edge thickness or side faces
gate: The linework is a single-weight wobbly BLACK ink line only; no coloured linework, no varying stroke weight.
Fills are flat ivory-cream only; no shading, no gradients, no painterly texture anywhere.
The ground is ONE flat colour from the pack palette, with at most a subtle paper grain.
Everything is face-on and flat: no perspective, no isometric, no parallelograms, no side faces or edge thickness.
At most 4 elements, with generous negative space; it does not read as a busy scene.
There is NO text, no letters, no numbers, and no UI chrome anywhere in the image.
Any hands are loopy and long-fingered and clearly non-anatomical; this look has no realistic finger-count to get wrong.
Any face is a single-line profile observing the work, never a rendered or detailed face.
